content.text: DenyAction is a policy effect introduced specifically to block DELETE operations. The standard Deny effect blocks resource creation and modification (PUT, PATCH) but cannot intercept delete requests — a critical gap when you need to protect production resources such as Log Analytics workspaces, Key Vaults, or network security groups from accidental or malicious deletion. DenyAction fills this gap by evaluating against delete operations and returning a 403 if the policy condition matches.

attrs.alt: DenyAction vs Deny vs Disabled operation coverage comparison

content.text: DenyAction only fires on DELETE operations. It does not affect create or update paths. If you need to prevent both creation of non-compliant resources and deletion of protected resources, you combine a Deny policy (for create/update) with a DenyAction policy (for delete) as separate assignments or separate policy definitions inside an initiative.

DenyAction uses a details block with a cascadeBehaviors property that controls what happens when a parent resource is deleted and the platform issues cascading deletes against child resources. Setting cascadeBehaviors to deny causes DenyAction to also block those cascading deletes, protecting child resources even when the parent is targeted.

DenyAction's cascadeBehaviors: resourceGroup: deny prevents the policy-protected resource from being deleted even when someone deletes its parent resource group. Without this setting, a resource group deletion would succeed for the child resource even if a DenyAction policy is applied.

# Create DenyAction policy definition
az policy definition create \
  --name protect-prod-law-workspaces \
  --display-name 'Prevent deletion of prod Log Analytics workspaces' \
  --rules @law-denyaction.json \
  --mode All \
  --management-group mg-corp

# Assign at management group scope
az policy assignment create \
  --name protect-law-ws \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/protect-prod-law-workspaces \
  --params '{"workspaceNamePrefix":{"value":"law-prod"}}'

# Test: attempt deletion of a protected workspace (expect 403)
az monitor log-analytics workspace delete \
  --resource-group rg-monitoring \
  --workspace-name law-prod-central \
  --yes
# Expected error:
# (RequestDisallowedByPolicy) Resource 'law-prod-central' was disallowed by policy.
# PolicyDefinitionName: protect-prod-law-workspaces
# PolicyAssignmentName: protect-law-ws

# Test cascading: attempt to delete the resource group (expect 403 for workspace)
az group delete --name rg-monitoring --yes
# The resource group delete will fail because the workspace inside it is protected
# Other resources in the RG may or may not be deleted depending on order

# To delete the workspace legitimately, you must first remove or exempt the assignment
az policy assignment delete \
  --name protect-law-ws \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp
# Then retry the delete
Central audit log sink — deletion destroys audit trail
Secrets repository — deletion causes application outages
Baseline network controls — deletion exposes subnets
Hub VNet — deletion severs all peering connections
Security automation — deletion removes alert forwarding
Governance guardrail — deletion removes compliance enforcement

Disabled is the simplest policy effect — it tells the policy engine to skip evaluation entirely for this definition. No compliance state is recorded, no operations are blocked, and no auditing occurs. The assignment remains in place and can be switched back to an active effect at any time without recreating the assignment.

The primary purpose of Disabled is to provide a fast, reversible off switch for a policy without deleting the assignment or creating a separate exempt. Use it in initiatives via an effect parameter so individual policies within the initiative can be suppressed for specific subscriptions or environments without modifying the initiative definition itself.
Using Disabled for Emergency Bypass and Rollout Stages

When a policy unexpectedly blocks a critical deployment, the fastest resolution without deleting the assignment is to change the effect parameter value to Disabled. This requires only an assignment update, which takes effect within a few minutes, and the assignment can be restored to Deny or Audit once the emergency is resolved. This preserves the assignment scope, parameters, and exemptions — nothing is lost.

# Emergency: policy is blocking a critical deployment in production
# Step 1: Switch effect to Disabled (fast — no assignment delete/recreate)
az policy assignment update \
  --name require-vm-skus \
  --scope /subscriptions/$PROD_SUB_ID \
  --set 'parameters.effect.value=Disabled'

# Step 2: Run the blocked deployment
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --template-file critical-vm.json

# Step 3: Restore enforcement once deployment is complete
az policy assignment update \
  --name require-vm-skus \
  --scope /subscriptions/$PROD_SUB_ID \
  --set 'parameters.effect.value=Deny'

# Staged rollout: initiative assignment overrides for different environments
# Dev subscription: all effects Disabled (total freedom to iterate)
az policy assignment create \
  --name corp-baseline-dev \
  --subscription $DEV_SUB_ID \
  --policy-set-definition $INITIATIVE_ID \
  --params '{
    "requireVmSkusEffect": {"value": "Disabled"},
    "requireEncryptionEffect": {"value": "Audit"},
    "denyPublicStorageEffect": {"value": "Disabled"}
  }'

# Staging subscription: Audit on volatile policies, Deny on critical ones
az policy assignment create \
  --name corp-baseline-staging \
  --subscription $STAGING_SUB_ID \
  --policy-set-definition $INITIATIVE_ID \
  --params '{
    "requireVmSkusEffect": {"value": "Audit"},
    "requireEncryptionEffect": {"value": "Deny"},
    "denyPublicStorageEffect": {"value": "Deny"}
  }'

# Production: full Deny enforcement
az policy assignment create \
  --name corp-baseline-prod \
  --subscription $PROD_SUB_ID \
  --policy-set-definition $INITIATIVE_ID \
  --params '{
    "requireVmSkusEffect": {"value": "Deny"},
    "requireEncryptionEffect": {"value": "Deny"},
    "denyPublicStorageEffect": {"value": "Deny"}
  }'
Terraform: DenyAction and Disabled in Practice
Compliance Behavior: Disabled vs Active Effects

When a policy effect is set to Disabled, the policy engine skips the definition entirely — no compliance scan runs, no PolicyStateChanged events are emitted, and all resources within scope show as Not Started in the compliance dashboard. This is different from an Exemption, which still evaluates the resource but marks it as Exempt. Disabled means no evaluation at all.
DenyAction vs RBAC Locks vs Resource Locks

DenyAction, Azure RBAC, and Azure Resource Locks all protect resources from deletion, but through different mechanisms and with different override paths. DenyAction is a policy effect — it fires on every delete request regardless of the caller's RBAC permissions, and can only be bypassed by removing or disabling the policy assignment. Azure Resource Locks (CanNotDelete) work at the ARM layer outside of policy and are controlled by RBAC on the lock itself. Understanding which layer to use matters for governance design.
Yes — if owner can delete the lock
Yes — policy assigned at MG scope
No — lock lives on each resource
Activity log (lock deleted then resource deleted)
Per-team explicit lock on specific resources
Remove delete permissions from a role

DenyAction policies can be included in initiatives alongside Deny, Audit, and AINE policies. Within an initiative, DenyAction definitions are parameterized with their own effect parameter, allowing them to be selectively disabled without touching other policies in the same initiative. This lets a platform team ship a single initiative assignment per subscription tier while toggling individual DenyAction policies off for resource groups that legitimately need deletion capability.
Monitoring Blocked Deletions with Activity Logs

Every delete request blocked by DenyAction generates an activity log entry with operation status Denied. These entries appear in the subscription activity log under the resource type that was targeted, with the caller's identity and the policy that blocked the request. You can create Azure Monitor alert rules on this activity to notify the platform team whenever someone attempts to delete a protected resource.

# Find all delete-blocked events in the last 24 hours across a subscription
az monitor activity-log list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '24 hours ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ 2>/dev/null || date -u -v-24H +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --status Failed \
  --query "[?contains(operationName.value, 'delete') && statusCode.value == 'Forbidden']
         .{Time:eventTimestamp, Op:operationName.value, Caller:caller, Resource:resourceId}" \
  -o table

# Create an alert rule that fires when DenyAction blocks a deletion
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --name alert-denyaction-blocked-deletion \
  --resource-group rg-monitoring \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --condition category=Policy and level=Error \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --action-group /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-monitoring/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/ag-platform-oncall

# Use Resource Graph to query policy denial events at scale
az graph query -q "
ResourceContainerResources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.insights/activitylogalerts'
| where properties.scopes contains '$SUB_ID'
" --subscription $SUB_ID

# Check compliance state for the DenyAction assignment
# Note: DenyAction does not generate Compliant/NonCompliant states the same way Deny does
# It records a policy violation event in the activity log each time it fires
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'protect-law-ws'" \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId,State:complianceState,Action:policyDefinitionAction}' \
  -o table
Disabled Effect and Compliance Scanning Costs

One operational consideration when using Disabled broadly is that the policy engine still counts disabled policies when reporting initiative compliance percentages. A policy with effect=Disabled shows resources as Not Started, which is distinct from Compliant and can skew initiative-level compliance dashboards. For initiatives that include dozens of definitions, Disabled policies create noise unless filtered out. The compliance query should include a filter to exclude Not Started resources from percentage calculations.

# List all policy assignments where the effect is currently Disabled
az policy state list \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'Not Started'" \
  --query '[].{Assignment:policyAssignmentName,Resource:resourceId}' \
  -o table

# Summarize initiative compliance excluding Not Started resources
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "policySetDefinitionId eq '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/corp-baseline'" \
  --query 'policyDefinitions[?results.queryResultsUri != null].{
    Policy:policyDefinitionId,
    Compliant:results.resourceDetails[?complianceState==\`Compliant\`].count | [0],
    NonCompliant:results.resourceDetails[?complianceState==\`NonCompliant\`].count | [0]
  }' \
  -o table

# Resource Graph: count Disabled vs active policies in all assignments
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments'
| where isnotnull(properties.parameters.effect)
| summarize
    Active   = countif(properties.parameters.effect.value != 'Disabled'),
    Disabled = countif(properties.parameters.effect.value == 'Disabled')
    by assignment = name
| where Disabled > 0
| order by Disabled desc
" --management-groups mg-corp

# Track when Disabled policies were last changed (to avoid forgetting to re-enable)
az monitor activity-log list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --caller $(az account show --query user.name -o tsv) \
  --query "[?operationName.value == 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/write']
         .{Time:eventTimestamp, Name:resourceId}" \
  -o table

DenyAction extends Azure Policy to the delete operation — filling the gap that Deny leaves since Deny only blocks create and update operations. Use DenyAction with cascadeBehaviors: resourceGroup: deny to protect critical infrastructure resources from both direct deletion and parent resource group deletion. Disabled is the inverse — a zero-cost off switch that suspends evaluation without removing the assignment, enabling staged rollouts and emergency bypasses through a simple effect parameter change.
